title: Changes to container runlabel for toolbox project
body:
The toolbox project would benefit from a few changes to more closely
resembe the original atomic cli project.  Changes made are:

* only pull image for container runlabel if the label exists in the image
* if a container image does not have the desired label, exit with non-zero

Signed-off-by: baude <bbaude@redhat.com>

comments.body: addresses @mtrmac comments and also added two tests for regression.

> The code looks good to me but I don't like adding the `label` parameter. It seems like a corner case that determines the API. I would prefer to make `checkRemoteImageForLabel` public and call it before pulling the image; or just copy the code.

The tricky part about is is all the heuristics involved in converting the user input into a `types.ImageReference`; we really don’t want to invent a new different set of heuristics.

It would be possible to make the `pullGoal` determination a public function, or move the “get and check label” code into the `image` subpackage.

What is best, I think, depends on whether this is really a check within an existing pull process, or a basically standalone read/inspect that does not typically involve pulling an entire image.

> Besides the pure design arguments, I would only change libpod's API in extreme cases to allow potential users to update without having API breakage.

(Does libpod have an API stability goal? I have no idea, so far I was relying on the repo owners to handle that.)

> What is best, I think, depends on whether this is really a check within an existing pull process, or a basically standalone read/inspect that does not typically involve pulling an entire image.

In my opinion, that should be a standalone check to avoid breaking the API. If API breakage is acceptable, I strongly encourage to encapsulate the label into a more generic struct (e.g., a `type ImageProperties struct`) to avoid adding more parameters in the future.

> (Does libpod have an API stability goal? I have no idea, so far I was relying on the repo owners to handle that.)

I don't know of a public statement :) But I know of tools using libpod directly instead of shelling out to Podman (wrote one at my former employer).

> In my opinion, that should be a standalone check to avoid breaking the API. If API breakage is acceptable, I strongly encourage to encapsulate the label into a more generic struct (e.g., a `type ImageProperties struct`) to avoid adding more parameters in the future.

Buildah has `PullOptions` FWIW; if we have such a generic `New` method, being explicit about what the options are for could be nice. Or maybe we do want a very generic `NewOptions` so that the API needs to never change? I don’t know, on balance I’d rather make it difficult to stuff too many very different things in there.

BTW not breaking the API does not inherently require the check to be stand-alone (if that meant low-performing): we can introduce a `NewWithOptions`, and keep the existing `New` as a compatibility wrapper that calls `NewWithOptions(…, {})`.

I  initially had it being separate but as @mtrmac says, the heuristics are proven to be correct right here.  I would be fine with breaking it out again so lets make a decision.  This needs to get in for the toolbox folks.

Don't we have a mechanism to pull down the entire json file that you see with podmain inspect,  I believe is what skopeo inspect is supposed to do.  In this case podman should pull down the image.json file and look for whatever labels he cares about.

> Don't we have a mechanism to pull down the entire json file that you see with podmain inspect, I believe is what skopeo inspect is supposed to do. In this case podman should pull down the image.json file and look for whatever labels he cares about.

That’s what this PR is doing, and that’s the easy part.

The non-obvious part is handling all the random forms of user input, incl. `docker-archive:foo.tar`, `docker://busybox` (meaning `docker.io/library/…`), `busybox` (meaning “search in the configured registries), … that are accepted for `podman {pull,run,create,…}` exactly the same way, and structuring the code reuse to be maintainable.
before this bitrots,can we merge or suggest a realistic alternative?
